ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tofu food product entry; no specific shredded tofu salad entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soybean curd (tofu) as the closest parent; no specific shredded tofu salad composite code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 covers tofu as a substance; no specific shredded tofu salad concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a small plate of shredded tofu salad as typically served in a Chinese restaurant or hawker setting.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 16126)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw firm tofu (NDB 16126 / FDC ID 172475), adjusted upward for sodium to reflect typical Chinese cold-dressed salad seasoning (soy sauce, sesame oil, vinegar). Energy and macros are primarily tofu-derived. Sodium is estimated at 350 mg per 100 g to account for soy sauce dressing typical of this preparation. This is an adapted estimate; no exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for shredded tofu salad was identified.

gi_source.title: Tofu, GI value from peer-reviewed literature — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008). International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for shredded tofu salad exists. GI of 15 is based on tofu as reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care), which lists soy products including tofu at GI approximately 15 (low). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 3.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 1.1. This is a low-GI food due to minimal digestible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.guidance: Shredded tofu salad can be moderate in sodium (approximately 350 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium dressing or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low digestible carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, shredded tofu salad has a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High plant protein, low saturated fat, and soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tofu provides plant-based protein and soy isoflavones with low saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g), which may support LDL cholesterol reduction; however, sodium from dressing should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shredded Tofu Salad (豆腐丝拌菜 / 凉拌豆腐丝) is a Chinese cold dish made from firm tofu that has been cut or shredded into thin strips and dressed with a seasoning mixture typically including soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar, minced garlic, chilli, spring onion, and coriander. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is a common appetiser or side dish in Chinese cuisine, particularly in northern and Sichuan-influenced cooking. The texture is soft yet slightly firm, with a savoury, umami-rich, mildly spicy flavour profile. Nutritionally, shredded tofu salad is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate, low-GI food. Per 100 g it provides approximately 94 kcal, 8.1 g protein, 5.0 g fat, and only 3.5 g carbohydrate, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following a low-glycaemic diet. The soy-based protein and isoflavone content may offer cardiovascular benefits. Sodium content is moderate due to soy sauce dressing and should be considered by individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free if tamari is substituted for soy sauce. It is commonly found in Chinese restaurants, hawker stalls, and home cooking across Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tofu salad?
Is tofu salad good for diabetics?
How much protein in tofu salad?
Can I eat tofu salad if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for shredded tofu salad was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw firm tofu with sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical soy sauce dressing. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (tofu) per Atkinson et al. 2008 in Diabetes Care. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172475, firm raw tofu) with sodium estimated at 350 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce dressing typical of Chinese cold tofu salad preparations. No exact composite dish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases for this specific dish. Sodium_mg is an estimate and may vary significantly depending on the amount of soy sauce used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plain tofu per USDA); cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero for tofu per USDA); vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients); vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as tofu is not a significant source of vitamin D and no fortification is assumed for this dish). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for firm raw tofu. GI source is analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for tofu/soy products at GI ~15. This dish is served at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles in Singapore (field survey source record). The cuisine_tags in the input (Drinks & Beverages, Noodles) appear to be restaurant-level tags unrelated to this specific dish and have been disregarded.
